turns.content: I'm building a CLI tool for my garden app and I keep getting confused about how to handle command-line flags versus environment variables. Specifically, if someone passes --watering true on the command line, should that always override whatever is in my .env file loaded by dotenvy?

You've hit the core of clap's design philosophy: precedence matters heavily here. When you use clap with env(), it reads from environment variables first, but if a command-line argument exists for that flag, it takes priority over everything else including your .env file loaded by dotenvy. So yes, --watering true will always win regardless of what's in the config or env vars. This is because clap treats CLI arguments as explicit user intent while env() just provides defaults when no args are given.

I've read about the precedence rules in clap and how flags override environment variables, but I still don't get why my CI pipeline keeps failing when I try to use dotenvy. It seems like my CLI arguments are supposed to take priority over env vars, so why isn't that working?

The issue is likely that you haven't properly configured the fallback chain in your builder. When you set up a Command with clap, the order of precedence is strictly flag first, then environment variables via the Env trait, and finally defaults. If your CI environment has variables set but they are being ignored, it usually means the command didn't register them correctly or there's a conflict between the CLI argument and the env var value.
That sounds logical on paper, but in my code I'm just calling env() and expecting dotenvy to load. Why is this failing? Is dotenvy actually part of the standard clap flow?

dotenvy isn't a built-in trait for clap; you must manually implement it by creating an instance that reads your .env file and then implementing the Env trait for that structure so clap can read from it. The real problem is that without this implementation, clap will never see those variables because there's no bridge between your custom struct and the CLI framework.
So I need to write a separate module to handle the .env file? That seems like extra work if I just want environment variable support.

Exactly, you have to implement the Env trait yourself because clap doesn't magically know about your custom types. You'll create a struct that holds your loaded configuration and then implement the get_env_var method so clap can query it at runtime. This is necessary to ensure your application respects the precedence order where flags override env vars which override defaults.
Okay, I'll try implementing the trait manually. But wait, if my CI sets a variable like RUST_LOG_LEVEL and I also have a CLI flag for --log-level, shouldn't that always win? Why would it fail?

It should always win because clap's precedence is strictly defined: command-line arguments are evaluated first and take absolute priority over any environment variables or defaults. If your pipeline is failing despite this order, you're probably not actually parsing the CLI argument correctly, or perhaps your test setup isn't passing the flag through the same way it passes env vars to the process.

I'm building a CLI tool with clap and I've got this weird precedence issue where my flag overrides everything but sometimes it feels like the environment variable is getting ignored. Can you walk me through how env() interacts with dotenvy and what happens when I set a default value?

When you use env(), clap reads from the current process environment, but if you want to load variables from a file like .env, you need to use the dotenvy crate to populate that environment before running your application. The precedence order is actually flag > env > default, meaning a command-line argument will always override both the environment variable and any default value you specify in your struct definition.
Wait, so if I set a default in my clap struct, can that ever be overridden by an env var?

That's the tricky part. The precedence is flag first, then environment variables, then defaults last. So yes, your env() call will override the default value you defined in the struct, but only if that environment variable actually exists in the process environment after dotenvy has loaded it.
